content: hi does the entire repo need to be cloned in order to try it out?
or will a shallow clone do?
Shallow clone should be fine

Personally, I'd recommend a blobless clone, to do it just pass invoke git using git clone --filter=blob:none, another option is the treeless clone, git clone --filter=tree:0, I do not encourage shallow clones as they are harder to work with.

Hi folks,

I'm using the quick start guide to build the kernel with Rust support (tip of "Rust" branch)

https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust/Documentation/rust/quick-start.rst

But unable to make it work.

Basically I followed all the instructions there and I can confirm make LLVM=1 rustavailable shows "Rust is available". But the problem is when I do make xconfig I couldn't find Rust support on the "General Setup" (please find the image attached).

Also when I search for Rust in the xconfig menu, it shows following details (also find it in the image attached).

Symbol: RUST [=n]
Type : bool
Defined at init/Kconfig:1919
Prompt: Rust support
Depends on: HAVE_RUST [=y] && RUST_IS_AVAILABLE [=y] && !MODVERSIONS [=n] && !GCC_PLUGINS [=y] && !RANDSTRUCT [=n] && !DEBUG_INFO_BTF [=n]
Location:
Main menu
-> General setup
Selects: CONSTRUCTORS [=n]

My guess from the above details

HAVE_RUST [=y] && RUST_IS_AVAILABLE [=y] && !MODVERSIONS [=n] && !GCC_PLUGINS [=y] && !RANDSTRUCT [=n] && !DEBUG_INFO_BTF [=n]

is that some of the config flags have value "n" ( !MODVERSIONS [=n] , !RANDSTRUCT [=n], !DEBUG_INFO_BTF [=n]) which eventually makes RUST config also to "n"?

Things tried so far.
==============

  1. build with RUST_CONFIG=1 make xconfig
  2. When I run scripts/rust_is_available.sh manually, first it complains that the compiler is not found because some ENV(s) were not set, So I ran it again after setting $CC, $RUSTC, $BINDGEN. And script able to return success (0) exit code.
linux$ ./scripts/rust_is_available.sh -v
linux$ echo $?
0

More details about the tools and kernel I'm using
=====================================

linux$ rustc --version
rustc 1.62.0 (a8314ef7d 2022-06-27)
linux$ bindgen --version
bindgen 0.56.0
linux$ clang --version
clang version 14.0.6
Target: x86_64-pc-linux-gnu
Thread model: posix
InstalledDir: /usr/bin
linux$ uname -a
Linux kaviraj-meerkat 5.15.60-1-MANJARO #1 SMP PREEMPT Thu A [Screenshot_2022-09-04_10-24-37.png](/user_uploads/35136/NqpBJsjnPfKJcUH7DjUA0jRo/Screenshot_2022-09-04_10-24-37.png) [Screenshot_2022-09-04_10-23-04.png](/user_uploads/35136/X_Mzg69dL0_eafTZWG-3Zv1D/Screenshot_2022-09-04_10-23-04.png) ug 11 13:14:05 UTC 2022 x86_64 GNU/Linux
linux$ cargo --version
cargo 1.62.0 (a748cf5a3 2022-06-08)

Has anyone faced the same or similar issue? Any help would be appreciated. Thanks in advance :)
What happens when you run make LLVM=1 xconfig?
Thanks Wedson. that worked. make LLVM=1 xconfig shows the Rust support under "general setup". Silly I missed that. I think it's worth adding it on the quick start guide? (I can send the PR)
https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/876 @Wedson Almeida Filho

is that some of the config flags have value "n" ( !MODVERSIONS [=n] , !RANDSTRUCT [=n], !DEBUG_INFO_BTF [=n]) which eventually makes RUST config also to "n"?

Almost! The problem is !GCC_PLUGINS [=y], i.e. the result is n. So if you disabled GCC plugin support, you should see the option appear.

Having said that, Wedson's suggestion (LLVM=1) is what you should do unless you have a good reason not to, because GCC support is just best-effort at this point in time :)

Kaviraj Kanagaraj said:

Thanks Wedson. that worked. make LLVM=1 xconfig shows the Rust support under "general setup". Silly I missed that. I think it's worth adding it on the quick start guide? (I can send the PR)

It is mentioned a bit below in the document, but it is true that we could move it up so that one sees it before the configuration step.

Hi, I'm currently the first to enter because I want to contribute to the Rust for Linux kernel. But I'm wandering because I don't have much experience in kernel development. What should I aim for first?

There is some good first issue on github:
https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues?q=is%3Aopen+is%3Aissue+label%3A%22good+first+issue%22
Thanks!
Please see https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing as well.

Thank you for the information. I'm currently looking at https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/1004 on this issue. I'm trying to implement some missing code for this file system abstraction and send PR. Is this work appropriate for me to be the first?
This could be a bit difficult for me now, but I would definitely like to try it..
maybe you could start looking through the patches I sent for the puzzlefs driver: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20230609063118.24852-1-amiculas@cisco.com/T/
https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/compare/rust-next...ariel-miculas:puzzlefs_rfc_v1

Hello everyone
I'd like to make a simple kernel module in Rust for Linux 6.3 as a personnal project.
Which repository should I clone: "torvalds/linux" or "Rust-for-Linux/linux" ?
I saw that "Rust-for-Linux/linux" was quite a few commits behind "torvalds/linux", but maybe it's not important ?
See <https://rust-for-linux.com/branches> and other pages on that website.

So, I should base my project on a fork of "torvalds/linux" then ?
Which documentation does that correspond to ?
I see rustdoc code docs (v6.3-rc2), rustdoc code docs (rust 2023-03-13), rustdoc code docs (pinned-init) and rustdoc code docs (pre-merge v8 patch).

We will have soon the docs generated in a subdomain at kernel.org. Meanwhile, you can get an up-to-date version locally via make rustdocs.

As for what to fork, I would recommend starting on top of the latest Linus' tag.

Took me a while to figure it out, it's make rustdoc on my fork, in "rust/Makefile". Is this on purpose ?
I see that the other commands to build the documentation are make htmldocs and make pdfdocs

Is this on purpose ?

If you mean the singular, yes, it is. The ones ending in docs are for the main (all) docs and do not require a .config.

I managed to build the documentation, it looks nice.
There are several broken links to std tough, would you like a PR to address this ?

Yes, of course! We use the mailing list in the kernel -- if you have not seen it, please read https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing.

You can also create an issue in GitHub, if you don't plan to send the patch(es) soon, to avoid forgetting about it.
Ah yes of course, kernel development is not as simple as the usual
So the plan is that over time, all the features behind the samples in the branch rust will also be available in mainline ?

More or less: some APIs have substantially changed already because a better approach was found while upstreaming them. Others that have not been upstreamed were WIPs/experimental, so they willl likely change too.
